text:
University College Hospital 

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Priya Nair  
Riverside Medical Practice  
12 Wharf Road, London N1 7RW  

Dear Dr Priya Nair,  

Daniel McBride, DOB: 22-07-1984, Hospital No: U908172 NHS No. 3015582294  
Flat 3, 4 Linton Court, London, E8 3PQ 

Summary/ Diagnoses: Combined generalised and focal epilepsy (patient reports both generalised tonic-clonic seizures and focal impaired-awareness events); Type 2 diabetes managed in primary care; mild peripheral neuropathic symptoms likely diabetes-related; ex-smoker. 

Medication: 
- Levetiracetam 1500 mg twice daily. 
- Lamotrigine 100 mg twice daily. 
- Metformin 1 g twice daily (primary care). 
- Atorvastatin 20 mg nocte (primary care). 

I reviewed Daniel McBride in neurology clinic today for epilepsy follow-up. He attended alone. He describes two seizure phenotypes: (1) focal impaired-awareness episodes with behavioural arrest and lip-smacking lasting 30–60 seconds, occasionally with right-hand automatisms; and (2) generalised tonic–clonic seizures that have typically occurred out of sleep without warning. There is no known family history of epilepsy. He denies head injury, CNS infection, or perinatal complications. Sleep is variable due to shift work. Alcohol minimal; no recreational drugs. 

Seizures: Since medication optimisation in June, he reports that events now come in short flurries, typically later in the day: about five brief focal episodes per cluster, usually in the early evening, with clusters occurring roughly every 2–3 weeks. He has had one generalised tonic–clonic seizure in the past four months, also in the evening after a missed lunch and poor sleep. No injuries reported. His partner has kept a simple log that broadly corroborates this pattern. There is no clear photic trigger; stress and delayed meals appear contributory. 

Examination: Alert and oriented. Speech fluent. Cranial nerves intact. Motor power full throughout, tone normal. Sensation: reduced vibration at toes bilaterally, consistent with diabetic peripheral neuropathy. Gait normal. No clinical focal deficits. 

Investigations: Prior MRI brain (epilepsy protocol, 2023) normal. Routine EEG (2023) showed intermittent generalised spike–wave discharges with occasional left temporal sharp waves. No recent bloods available in our system today. 

Impression: Combined generalised and focal epilepsy, with both generalised tonic–clonic seizures and focal impaired-awareness seizures persisting but improved in frequency and severity since adjustment to levetiracetam and lamotrigine. Evening clustering of brief focal events remains the main issue. Type 2 diabetes continues to be overseen in primary care; mild distal sensory loss likely diabetic. 

Plan: 
- Increase lamotrigine to 125 mg twice daily for two weeks, then 150 mg twice daily if tolerated; maintain levetiracetam 1500 mg twice daily. 
- Safety: advised on seizure first aid, SUDEP risk discussion undertaken; avoid swimming alone and heights; no driving—he understands DVLA requirements and will inform DVLA if any further generalised events occur. 
- Lifestyle: emphasised regular meals and sleep hygiene given evening clustering; continue seizure diary noting timing relative to meals, sleep, and stress. 
- Bloods: request U&E, LFT, FBC, and lamotrigine level via GP in 2–3 weeks after dose change; HbA1c per diabetes review as scheduled. 
- Liaison: primary care to continue diabetes management; please consider foot care referral if neuropathic symptoms progress. 
- Follow-up: neurology review in 4 months, sooner if generalised seizures recur or clusters increase in frequency. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: about five brief focal episodes per cluster, usually in the early evening, with clusters occurring roughly every 2–3 weeks.

attributes.Label: 1 cluster per 2 to 3 weeks, 5 per cluster

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter describes two seizure types with frequencies. For focal impaired-awareness events: "about five brief focal episodes per cluster" with "clusters occurring roughly every 2–3 weeks." For generalised tonic–clonic seizures: "one ... in the past four months." Per the rule to select the highest frequency, the focal clusters are more frequent than 1 per 4 months. The patient is not seizure free (events ongoing), so we provide the cluster format: 1 cluster per 2 to 3 weeks, 5 per cluster.
seizure_frequency_number: 1 cluster per 2 to 3 weeks, 5 per cluster
about five brief focal episodes per cluster, usually in the early evening, with clusters occurring roughly every 2–3 weeks.
reference: Per-cluster load around 5 brief events; timing tends to be evenings

flaws.description: Gold (1 cluster per 2 to 3 weeks, 5 per cluster) disagrees with Reference (unknown)
